title: B2 Grammar: Mixed Practice
intro_rule: At B2 level, accuracy depends on mastering three areas: (1) perfect and conditional tenses for past events and hypotheticals, (2) correct prepositions in fixed collocations, and (3) uncountable nouns that have no plural form.
intro_examples: By the time she arrived, the meeting had already started. (past perfect for the earlier event)
If I had studied more, I would have passed. (third conditional — not 'If I would have...')
The advice was helpful. (not 'advices' — 'advice' is uncountable)

exercises.prompt: By the time the ambulance arrived, the patient ___ already regained consciousness.
exercises.hint: Past perfect: had + past participle. Use when one past action preceded another.

feedback.explanation: Past perfect ('had + past participle') marks the earlier of two past actions. Here, regaining consciousness happened before the ambulance arrived.
feedback.practical_tip: When two past events are described together, the one that happened first needs 'had': 'By the time X happened, Y had already occurred.'

exercises.prompt: The government is responsible ___ protecting its citizens from harm.
exercises.hint: Responsible always collocates with 'for' when followed by a noun or gerund.

feedback.explanation: 'Responsible' is always followed by 'for' — it is a fixed collocation. 'Responsible to protect' or 'responsible of protecting' are both incorrect.
feedback.practical_tip: Memorise the trio: responsible for, accountable for, in charge of. All express obligation and all use 'for' or 'of', never 'to' or 'about'.

exercises.prompt: The doctor suggested ___ less caffeine to improve sleep quality.
exercises.hint: After 'suggest', use a gerund (-ing form), not an infinitive.

feedback.explanation: After 'suggest', English requires a gerund (-ing form). 'Suggest to drink' is a common learner error but is not standard British or American English.
feedback.practical_tip: After suggest, recommend, avoid, enjoy, and consider — always use -ing, never 'to + infinitive'. These verbs never take a to-infinitive directly.

exercises.prompt: He spoke ___ though he had never encountered the concept before.
exercises.hint: 'as though' and 'as if' introduce hypothetical comparisons. Supply the first word.

feedback.explanation: 'As though' is a two-word conjunction meaning 'in a way that suggests'. The first word is 'as'. Using only 'though' changes the meaning entirely.
feedback.practical_tip: 'As though' and 'as if' are interchangeable and both introduce hypothetical comparisons. After them, use past tense for present situations: 'He speaks as if he knew everything.'

exercises.prompt: The contract ___ by both parties before the deadline next Friday.

feedback.explanation: Future perfect passive ('will have been + past participle') is needed: the signing will be completed before a future deadline, and 'the contract' cannot sign itself.
feedback.practical_tip: Spot two clues: 'before next Friday' signals a future deadline (future perfect), and 'the contract' as subject signals passive voice. Both clues must be satisfied.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly expresses regret about a past event?

options.text: I wish I study harder for the exam.
I wish I studied harder for the exam.
I wish I had studied harder for the exam.
I wish I have studied harder for the exam.

feedback.explanation: To express regret about the past, use 'wish + past perfect'. 'I wish I had studied' expresses regret about something that is finished and cannot be changed.
feedback.practical_tip: Wish + past simple = present wish ('I wish I knew'). Wish + past perfect = past regret ('I wish I had known'). Never use 'would have' directly after 'wish'.

exercises.prompt: Despite ___ for the role, she did not receive a callback.

feedback.explanation: After 'despite', use a noun or gerund phrase, never a subject-verb clause. 'Her qualifying' is a possessive gerund — 'her' is the logical subject, 'qualifying' is the gerund.
feedback.practical_tip: After 'despite' and 'in spite of': use noun or -ing form only. You cannot use a full clause ('she qualified') directly after these prepositions.

exercises.prompt: She has been working at this company since ten years.
accepted_corrections: She has been working at this company for ten years.
She's been working at this company for ten years.
She has been working at this company for 10 years.
She has worked at this company for ten years.
She has been working at the company for ten years.

feedback.explanation: 'Since' introduces a specific point in time ('since 2015', 'since Monday'). 'Ten years' is a duration, so 'for' is correct: 'for ten years'.
feedback.practical_tip: Quick test: if you can say 'a period of ten years', use 'for'. If it names a starting moment (a date, an event), use 'since'. Duration = for; Starting point = since.

exercises.prompt: If I would have studied more, I would have passed the exam.
accepted_corrections: If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam.
If I'd studied more, I would have passed the exam.
If I had studied more, I would've passed the exam.
Had I studied more, I would have passed the exam.
If I had studied more I would have passed the exam.

feedback.explanation: In a third conditional, the 'if' clause uses past perfect ('had studied'), never 'would have'. 'Would have' belongs only in the result clause.
feedback.practical_tip: Pattern: 'If + had + past participle, ... would have + past participle.' A simple rule: 'would' never appears in the 'if' clause of any conditional.

exercises.prompt: The advices she gave me were very useful.
accepted_corrections: The advice she gave me was very useful.
The advice that she gave me was very useful.
The advice which she gave me was very useful.
The advice she gave me was very helpful.

feedback.explanation: 'Advice' is an uncountable noun in English and has no plural form. 'Advices' does not exist. The verb must also change: 'were' becomes 'was'.
feedback.practical_tip: Memorise these common uncountable nouns: advice, information, knowledge, research, furniture, equipment, progress, luggage. None take 's', and all use singular verbs.
